candidate_summary: estoppel-by-reliance (common-law doctrine) → runtime discipline for long-running LLM agents where prior claims acquire procedural lock-status once downstream steps rely on them, and retraction requires explicit cascade-recovery
reasoning: Mechanical rule: 7 hits, 2 forced (both legal source-domain reference pages, source-domain noise). Substantive prior art (agent-judged): AgentArmor (arxiv 2508.01249) introduces Program Dependence Graphs over agent runtime, with a dependency analyzer inferring data/control dependencies between steps — this IS the runtime mechanism I was proposing, just framed as security rather than estoppel. Graph-Augmented LLM Agents survey (2507.21407) characterizes the broader 'agentic workflow as graph with logical-dependency edges' field. Consistency-Score evaluation metrics in 2507.21504 and self-consistency methods in 2503.16416 cover the evaluation side. Verdict FAIL: the architectural mechanism (dependency graph over agent runtime + reliance edges) is published; estoppel as analogy frame is a relabeling rather than a new mechanism.
